Getting started with llegir

Source: vignettes/getting-started.Rmd
getting-started.Rmd

llegir (“to sense”, Catalan) is a Module Interpretation Engine: it loads a gene co-expression module object (starting with hdWGCNA), gathers a standardized bundle of evidence per module through a toolbox of deterministic R functions, and drafts an evidence-backed interpretation paragraph via a model-agnostic synthesis layer with faithfulness checking and confidence fusion.

This vignette walks the whole pipeline end to end on a small, self-contained synthetic dataset – no Seurat/hdWGCNA object, no external data file, and no network access or API key required. It builds entirely offline via [mock_backend()], so it runs the same way in R CMD check as it does interactively.

llegir is experimental: the public API, especially the ModuleSet adapter contract and the custom-tool interface, is expected to evolve before a 1.0 release.

1. A ModuleSet

Every core tool depends only on the ModuleSet adapter contract – [modules()], [gene_membership()], [module_scores()], [expression()], [metadata()], [pkg_versions()] – never on a specific backend package. [hdWGCNA_ModuleSet()] is the real-world adapter, built from a Seurat object that has been run through hdWGCNA. For this vignette we instead use [llegir_example_moduleset()], a small simulated dataset (two co-expressed gene modules plus background noise genes, 200 cells) that satisfies the same contract, so every tool below runs unmodified against it.

library(llegir)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'llegir'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     capabilities, expression

ms <- llegir_example_moduleset()
modules(ms)
#> [1] "module_a" "module_b"
head(gene_membership(ms, 'module_a'))
#>    gene_name   module       kme
#> 4     GENEA4 module_a 0.9389462
#> 9     GENEA9 module_a 0.9368404
#> 5     GENEA5 module_a 0.9330942
#> 10   GENEA10 module_a 0.9324491
#> 8     GENEA8 module_a 0.9304695
#> 1     GENEA1 module_a 0.9299657
head(metadata(ms))
#>       diagnosis cell_type  sample
#> cell1      case myeloid_b sample1
#> cell2   control myeloid_b sample1
#> cell3      case myeloid_a sample3
#> cell4      case myeloid_b sample2
#> cell5   control myeloid_b sample3
#> cell6      case myeloid_b sample4

module_a is simulated to associate with the diagnosis column, and module_b with cell_type – used below to show a real (not null) evidence signal.

2. Evidence tools

Each tool takes a ctx <- list(ms, module_id, params) and returns a single, validated evidence_fragment – see [evidence_fragment()] and the shipped evidence_fragment.schema.json for the full contract.

ctx <- list(ms = ms, module_id = 'module_a', params = list(n_hubs = 10))
hub_frag <- hub_genes_tool(ctx)
validate_evidence_fragment(hub_frag)
hub_frag$compact_summary
#> [1] "top 10 hub genes by kME: GENEA4, GENEA9, GENEA5, GENEA10, GENEA8, GENEA1, GENEA2, GENEA7, GENEA3, GENEA6"
meta_frag <- module_by_metadata_tool(list(
    ms = ms, module_id = 'module_a',
    params = list(column = 'diagnosis', column_type = 'categorical')
))
meta_frag$compact_summary
#> [1] "diagnosis (cell-level): strongest group case (r=0.8, FDR=9.5e-23); omnibus Kruskal p=9.4e-23"
cluster_frag <- cluster_dme_tool(list(
    ms = ms, module_id = 'module_b',
    params = list(group_by = 'cell_type')
))
cluster_frag$compact_summary
#> [1] "strongest state: myeloid_a (r=0.86, FDR=1.1e-25); omnibus Kruskal p=1.1e-25"

A fourth core tool, [geneset_enrichment_tool()], runs offline GO/pathway enrichment against a local GMT file (ctx$params$db_files); it’s omitted here since no gene-set library ships with the package, but it follows the same ctx -> evidence_fragment contract as the tools above.

User-supplied result tables (e.g. a DME table computed outside llegir) can be folded into the same pipeline via [import_fragment()] / [import_fragment_tool()], tagged provenance$source = 'user_supplied' rather than 'computed'.

3. Evidence packets

[run_module()] runs a configured list of tools against one module and bundles the fragments into a validated, hashed evidence packet – [build_evidence_packet()] under the hood. The packet_hash is a content hash of the fragments (timestamps excluded), so it’s a reproducibility fingerprint: the same evidence always hashes the same way.

tool_config <- list(
    list(fn = hub_genes_tool, params = list(n_hubs = 10)),
    list(fn = module_by_metadata_tool, params = list(column = 'diagnosis', column_type = 'categorical'))
)
packet <- run_module(ms, 'module_a', tool_config, input_hash = 'vignette-example')
packet$packet_hash
#> [1] "f4ced8450d61cbef823fbdd533dc32bb37b5803252ebc593d1510c23b3c25732"
length(packet$fragments)
#> [1] 2

[run_orchestrator()] does this for every module in a ModuleSet at once, writing one packet JSON per module (plus, optionally, every fragment’s full result table via tables_dir):

out_dir <- tempfile('packets_')
packets <- run_orchestrator(ms, tool_config, output_dir = out_dir)
list.files(out_dir)
#> [1] "module_a.json" "module_b.json"

A single module failing (e.g. a degenerate module tripping up one tool) doesn’t take down the whole batch – it’s warned and recorded as NULL, leaving every other module’s packet intact.

4. Dataset context

Synthesis requires a [dataset_description()] – the biological context prepended to every prompt, so the model interprets a module in the right frame (e.g. CSF myeloid vs. tumor tissue changes what a given program means). It’s a hard requirement, not a default: [validate_dataset_description()] errors on a missing/empty required field.

desc <- dataset_description(
    species = 'human', tissue = 'simulated tissue', cell_compartment = 'simulated cells',
    assay = 'simulated scRNA-seq', conditions = c('case', 'control')
)
cat(render_dataset_description(desc))
#> Dataset context:
#> - species: human
#> - tissue: simulated tissue
#> - cell compartment: simulated cells
#> - assay: simulated scRNA-seq
#> - conditions: case, control

5. Synthesis

[synthesize_module()] runs the full packet -> interpretation pipeline: calls a backend via [synthesize_interpretation()], enforces citation faithfulness ([enforce_faithfulness()] – every claim’s fragment_ids must actually exist in the packet, and its direction must match), and fuses model and deterministic confidence ([fuse_confidence()]).

The model’s role is bounded by design: it only ever sees the compact prompt built by [build_system_prompt()] / [build_user_prompt()] (fragment summaries and top findings, never raw result tables), and it never runs analysis code.

Here, [mock_backend()] stands in for a live model – a canned, fixed response that never touches the network, used by the whole test suite and by this vignette:

interp <- synthesize_module(packet, desc, backend = mock_backend())
interp$proposed_label
#> [1] "Myeloid activation program (mock)"
interp$confidence$score
#> [1] 0.5731577
cat(render_paragraph(interp))
#> **Myeloid activation program (mock)** (module_a)
#> 
#> Mock synthesis output for offline testing; not derived from the evidence packet.
#> 
#> Dominant biology: Not evaluated by the mock backend.
#> 
#> Supporting evidence:
#> - Hub genes were computed by the deterministic core. (hub_genes; direction: na)
#> - The module has enriched gene-set terms. (geneset_enrichment; direction: up)
#> 
#> Confidence: 0.57 -- Mock backend: fixed neutral confidence, not evidence-derived. [fusion: model=0.50, evidence=0.65 (effect=0.94, enrichment=0.00, agreement=1.00/convergent_signal), fused=0.57]
#> Flags: needs_human_review

Live backends

llegir’s synthesis backends are pluggable via [resolve_backend()] (provider = 'github' or 'gemini', both built on [ellmer_backend()]) or [ellmer_backend()] directly with any ellmer::chat_*() constructor. These need network access and a provider API key (picked up by ellmer itself from the provider’s environment variable, e.g. GITHUB_PAT or GEMINI_API_KEY), so they aren’t exercised in this vignette or in R CMD check – only shown here for reference:

backend <- resolve_backend(provider = 'gemini')
interp <- synthesize_module(packet, desc, backend = backend)

[cached_backend()] wraps any backend with an on-disk cache keyed on the packet hash + provider + model + prompt template version, so re-running synthesis over an unchanged packet never re-spends an API call.

6. Batch synthesis: review queue and manifest

[run_synthesis_orchestrator()] runs [synthesize_module()] over a batch of packets (e.g. the output of [run_orchestrator()]), writing per-module interpretation JSON and rendered Markdown paragraphs, plus a run-level review_queue.tsv and manifest.json:

synth_dir <- tempfile('synthesis_')
interps <- run_synthesis_orchestrator(packets, desc, backend = mock_backend(), output_dir = synth_dir)
list.files(synth_dir)
#> [1] "manifest.json"    "module_a.json"    "module_a.md"      "module_b.json"   
#> [5] "module_b.md"      "review_queue.tsv"

The review queue ([build_review_queue()]) lists only flagged interpretations ([needs_review()]) – a triage queue for a human reviewer, not a summary of every module – sorted lowest-confidence first:

build_review_queue(interps)
#>          module_id confidence              flags
#> module_b  module_b      0.491 needs_human_review
#> module_a  module_a      0.573 needs_human_review
#>                                                                                       reason
#> module_b model confidence and deterministic evidence disagree, or a citation issue was found
#> module_a model confidence and deterministic evidence disagree, or a citation issue was found

The mock backend always returns the same fixed, generic content regardless of the actual evidence packet – so its citations and confidence score are never truly grounded in this vignette’s per-module evidence, and needs_human_review is expected here. A live model, synthesizing from the real packet contents, will typically agree with the deterministic evidence more often and flag fewer modules.

On this page
